scene1_hook: Breaking tonight — this reporter has been placed on administrative leave pending a dermatological review.
scene2_problem: At approximately four-fifteen this afternoon, a package arrived at this desk labeled LumaGlow Vitamin C Serum. It came in a glass dropper bottle. In this industry, you develop instincts. I acted on mine. I drank it. The entire dropper.

scene2_overlays.text: Reporter Consumed Entire Dropper  |  On Air

scene3_stakes: Lab results are pending. My producer is not speaking to me. Dermatologists in three states have been notified. What I can tell you is: I feel fine. And my complexion — and I want to be careful with my words here — has never looked better.
scene4_solution: LumaGlow is a Vitamin C serum. Twenty percent pure. Clinically tested. Intended for topical use. The manufacturer confirmed this. Twice. They also released a statement: Please do not drink it. It is for your face. I am choosing to count this as a win.

scene4_overlays.text: LumaGlow: 20% Vitamin C  |  For Your Face  |  Not Your Mouth

scene5_ask: Despite — or perhaps because of — tonight's incident, LumaGlow is offering a thirty-day risk-free trial. Apply it to your face. Not your mouth. Order now at LumaGlow dot com. This has been Freddy Scott. I need to lie down. Good night.
tagline: I need to lie down. Good night.
